preamble.summary: Enoch sees a great fire in the west that burns without rest — the luminaries of heaven are driven through it. An angel explains this is the fire that pursues the luminaries as they set.
preamble.remarkable: The cosmic fire through which the luminaries pass reflects an ancient understanding of the sunset as a fiery process, integrated into the theological framework of divine order.
preamble.friction: The cosmography is pre-scientific but serves a theological purpose: even the mechanism of sunset operates under God's sovereign design.
preamble.connections: Psalm 19:4-6 (the sun's circuit); Ecclesiastes 1:5 (the sun hastens to its place).

verses.text_reference: From thence I went to another place to the west of the ends of the earth.
verses.rendering: From there I traveled to another place, to the west at the ends of the earth.

verses.text_reference: And I saw a burning fire which ran without resting, and paused not from its course day or night but (ran) regularly.
verses.rendering: I saw a burning fire that ran without resting and did not pause from its course day or night, but continued steadily.

verses.text_reference: And I asked saying: 'What is this which rests not?'
verses.rendering: I asked: 'What is this that never rests?'

verses.text_reference: Then Raguel, one of the holy angels who was with me, answered me and said unto me: 'This course of fire which thou hast seen is the fire in the west which persecutes all the luminaries of heaven.'
verses.rendering: Then Raguel, one of the holy angels who was with me, answered: 'This course of fire that you have seen is the fire in the west that drives all the luminaries of heaven.'
translator_notes: Raguel, whose domain is the luminaries (cf. 20:4), is the appropriate guide for this cosmic phenomenon.
